metadata.title: Why 'Improve This' Is a Wish, Not an Instruction

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Dr. Osei's problem was that 'improve this' left all six slots — task, context, sources, constraints, format, evaluation — for Claude to guess, and the context guess went wrong for her specialist committee. I have an intro aimed at a specialist review committee too, and I want to stop leaking generic guesses. Help me: (1) write out what each of the six slots should say for my specialist-audience intro; (2) flag which slot, if I fill it wrong, would do the most damage the way context did for her; (3) fold all six into a single tight prompt.

props.command: Dr. Osei's problem was that 'improve this' left all six slots — task, context, sources, constraints, format, evaluation — for Claude to guess, and the context guess went wrong for her specialist committee. I have an intro aimed at a specialist review committee too, and I want to stop leaking generic guesses. Help me: (1) write out what each of the six slots should say for my specialist-audience intro; (2) flag which slot, if I fill it wrong, would do the most damage the way context did for her; (3) fold all six into a single tight prompt.

beats.narration_text: She asked for an improved introduction. Claude improved it. The result was polished, well-organized, and wrong for her actual situation — because 'improve this' left six decisions to Claude, and Claude made six generic guesses.

card.copy: Why 'Improve this' is a wish, not an instruction
card.sub: six missing decisions, six generic guesses

beats.narration_text: Dr. Osei is writing for a specialist review committee — experts who know the field. She pastes her introduction and types: can you improve this introduction? Claude returns a polished rewrite organized for a general academic audience.
beats.scene_description: A researcher at a desk reading a document, looking puzzled at the output, editorial newsprint treatment
beats.image_prompt: B02, academic researcher at a desk, looking puzzled at a printed document, stacks of papers and books around her, editorial documentary newsprint treatment, desaturated flat print collage

beats.narration_text: She asked for an improved introduction. She got a polished rewrite — optimized for the wrong audience. Why did a well-meaning improvement produce the wrong output?

card.copy: Why did a well-meaning improvement produce the wrong output?

beats.narration_text: A prompt is a specification. It covers six slots: task, context, sources, constraints, format, and evaluation criteria. 'Improve this' answers exactly none of them — so Claude supplies its best generic guess for all six simultaneously.

The guesses are plausible. Task: make it clearer. Context: general academic reader. Sources: the text itself. Constraints: standard academic norms. Format: prose paragraphs. Evaluation: reads well. Each one is reasonable. None of them match Dr. Osei's situation.

The slot that matters most: context. Dr. Osei's actual context: a specialist committee that already knows the field. General-audience framing is exactly wrong. But she never told Claude who would read it.
The same input — the same raw text — produces two different outputs depending on what fills those six slots. The text is not the specification. The slots are the specification.
Each slot you fill removes a generic guess. Fill all six and Claude's guesses go to zero — the output is calibrated to your situation, not to a plausible default.

card.copy: Each filled slot removes a generic guess

beats.narration_text: Dr. Osei fills the slots: task — tighten the gap statement. Context — specialist review committee. Sources — only use evidence already in the text. Constraints — do not add claims. Format — one paragraph. Evaluation — gap must be clear in the first two sentences. Same raw text. Different output. Right this time.

The six slots apply to every request. Not just editing. Summarize this. Analyze this. Write me something. Every vague instruction leaves slots empty — and every empty slot is a generic guess you didn't make.

Jamie asks Claude to analyze this dataset. No context, no constraints, no format. Claude produces a summary of descriptive statistics — which is the most common analysis for a dataset. Jamie needed an outlier detection report. Those are different things. The slot for task said 'analyze.' That left the type of analysis to Claude.

Jamie fills the task slot: identify outliers, flag any value more than three standard deviations from the mean. One slot filled, five still open. But the one that mattered most — what kind of analysis — is now specified. The output is an outlier report.

Before you submit a vague request, spend thirty seconds on each slot. Task: what specific verb? Context: who reads this, in what situation? Sources: what should Claude draw from? Constraints: what must it not do? Format: what shape? Evaluation: how will you know it worked?
A prompt is a specification covering six slots. Leave them empty and Claude guesses. Fill them and Claude has something to aim at.

card.copy: A prompt is a work order with six slots.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Why 'Improve This' Is a Wish, Not an Instruction] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
